coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dot based nanocarrier labeled with anti-PDL1 antibody as a promising theranostic candidate for the triple negative breast cancer treatment
coredata.dc:creator: Azizi, M.
Sheini, A.
Seyed Dorraji, Mir Saeed
Alidadi, H.
Fekri, E.
coredata.dc:description: Triple-negative breast cancer is a significant health concern for women worldwide. With the development of optical and chemical methods, carbon dots (CDs), as a special class of carbon nanomaterials with unique physiochemical properties, have attracted the attention of researchers in topics such as tumor theranostics, especially bioimaging and targeted therapy. The goal of this research is to create theranostic nanoparticles (NPs)-antibody-conjugated systems that will improve cancer diagnostics and therapeutic effects. The CDs bioconjugate constituting carbon dots was prepared as an imaging agent and anti-PDL1 antibody as a targeting and immunotherapy agent. MTT test was used to evaluate the cell viability of MDA-MB-231 (breast cancer cells) exposed to anti-PD-L1-CD. The effect of anti-PD-L1-CD on proliferation and apoptosis was detected by colony assay and AO/EB assay, respectively. Our results demonstrated that CD could help to effective internalization of anti-PD-L1 antibodies into MDA-MB-231 cells and boost the cytotoxic impacts of anti-PD-L1 antibodies. Additionally, the study showed that anti-PD-L1-CD inhibited cell viability and proliferation of tumor cells more efficiently than free anti-PD-L1 antibody.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Breast cancer
Theranostic nanoparticles
Bioconjugate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Bioconjugation of anti-PDL1 antibody with CD by glutaraldehyde
Optical and component characterization of CD
Optical characterization of the bioconjugate CDs
Cell compatibility of the CDs bioconjugate

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: University of Zanjan and Shahid Chamran University of Ahvaz

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank the University of Zanjan and Shahid Chamran University of Ahvaz for their financial and other supports.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Vis spectra and fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs. (b) CD aqueous solution under visible light, (c) CD aqueous solution under UV light (380 nm).
TEM images of the samples (a) CDs (b) size of CDs.

Fluorescence emission spectra of: (A) CD-PEG-Glu, (B) CD-PEG, (C) differences in fluorescence emission intensity and (D) red shift in conjugated system emission. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

The impacts of a free CD, anti-PD-L1-CD, and anti-PD-L1 antibodies on the viability of MDA-MB-231 and HDF cells. (A) The morphology of MDA-MB-231 and HDF cells. (B) The impact of free C.dot on cell viability of HDF cells and MDA-MB-231 for 24 h. (C) The impact of anti-PD-L1-CD and anti-PD-L1 mAB on cell viability of HDF cells and (D) MDA-MB-231 cells for 24 h. The outcomes are expressed as Mean ± SD (n = 3). **P < 0.01 and ***P < 0.001; * symbol shows as compared with Anti PD-L1-CD and anti PD-L1 mAB.

Fluorescence images of MDA-MB-231 cells subjected to anti-PD-L1, anti-PD-L1-CD and DAPI. The blue color shows DAPI-stained nuclei and the green color shows CD. Scale bar: 100 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

The capability of colony formation of MDA-MB-231 cells subjected to anti-PD-L1-CD and anti-PD-L1 for 10 days. The outcomes are expressed as means ± SD (n = 3). ***p < 0.001; * symbol shows as compared with anti- PD-L1 mAB and anti-PD-L1-C.dot.

The cytotoxic effect of anti-PD-L1-CD and anti-PD-L1 on MDA-MB-231 cells for 24 h. (A) Live and dead cells. (B) The percentage of apoptotic cells in those of MDA-MB-231. The outcomes are expressed as means ± SD (n = 3). ***p < 0.001; * symbol shows as compared with anti-PD-L1-CD and anti-PD-L1. Scale bar: 100 μm.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dot based nanocarrier labeled with anti-PDL1 antibody as a promising theranostic candidate for the triple negative breast cancer treatment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Applied Chemistry Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Zanjan, Zanjan, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Applied Chemistry Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Zanjan, Zanjan, Iran.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Engineering, Shohadaye Hoveizeh Campus of Technology, Shahid Chamran University of Ahvaz, Ahvaz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Engineering, Shohadaye Hoveizeh Campus of Technology, Shahid Chamran University of Ahvaz, Ahvaz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cellular and Molecular Research Center, Medical Basic Sciences Research Institute, Ahvaz Jundishapur University of Medical Sciences, Ahvaz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ahvaz Jundishapur University of Medical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Cellular and Molecular Research Center, Medical Basic Sciences Research Institute, Ahvaz Jundishapur University of Medical Sciences, Ahvaz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Triple-negative breast cancer is a significant health concern for women worldwide. With the development of optical and chemical methods, carbon dots (CDs), as a special class of carbon nanomaterials with unique physiochemical properties, have attracted the attention of researchers in topics such as tumor theranostics, especially bioimaging and targeted therapy. The goal of this research is to create theranostic nanoparticles (NPs)-antibody-conjugated systems that will improve cancer diagnostics and therapeutic effects. The CDs bioconjugate constituting carbon dots was prepared as an imaging agent and anti-PDL1 antibody as a targeting and immunotherapy agent. MTT test was used to evaluate the cell viability of MDA-MB-231 (breast cancer cells) exposed to anti-PD-L1-CD. The effect of anti-PD-L1-CD on proliferation and apoptosis was detected by colony assay and AO/EB assay, respectively. Our results demonstrated that CD could help to effective internalization of anti-PD-L1 antibodies into MDA-MB-231 cells and boost the cytotoxic impacts of anti-PD-L1 antibodies. Additionally, the study showed that anti-PD-L1-CD inhibited cell viability and proliferation of tumor cells more efficiently than free anti-PD-L1 antibody.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data associated with this study are present in the paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Triple negative breast cancer (TNBC) is one of the most invasive types of breast cancer, which includes about 15–20 % of all known breast cancers, which can sometimes be very aggressive and have a poor prognosis [1]. TNBC is caused by the fact that cancer cells do not have progesterone receptors (PR), estrogen receptors (ER), and human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER-2) [2–4]. The lack of above-mentioned hormone receptors in tumor cells makes this type of cancer more difficult to treat and the probability of its recurrence increases. The treatment method is mostly based on chemotherapy [5,6]. Even planned chemotherapy based on approved drugs can lead to poor response, high toxicity and drug resistance. Also, molecular heterogeneity, visceral metastasis and BRCA mutations (BRCA*) cause poor treatment of this type of cancer [6–9]. Precision diagnosis is critical for early treatment intervention. Palpation, ultrasonography, mammography, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and immunohistochemistry (IHC) are now the best TNBC diagnosis in the clinical setting [10,11]. Wrong diagnoses occur as a result of non-specific use of contrast agents, false positive results, are some of the limiting factors in confirming and correctly diagnosing TNBC. Furthermore, treatment options are restricted to radiotherapy, surgery, and taxanes, as well as anthracyclines-based cytotoxic chemotherapy [12]. Such constraints negate the need to enhance presently offered diagnostic and therapeutic procedures, as well as to investigate novel approaches and methods. The advancement of nanotechnology in biomedical sciences in recent decades in the fields of contrast agents and drug delivery carriers has led to targeted and simultaneous delivery of diagnostic and therapeutic agents. A wide range of nano-carriers made of polymers, lipids, nucleic acid, carbon, proteins, and metals in the form of micelles, liposomes, dendrimers, nanoparticles/tubes are available today [13–17]. Such intelligent nanoparticles include anti-tumor medicines (arsenal). These nanoparticles have specific ligands on their surface that interact with the receptor expressed on the surface of the target molecule and eradicate the cells, enabling simultaneous cancer diagnosis and treatment. Theranostic approaches have grown increasingly commonly used in recent years to develop effective drug delivery systems that can bypass biological barriers and administer the right amount of medicine at the right site and at/for the right time, decreasing side effects and boosting therapeutic efficiency [18]. Treatment choices for TNBC are few, immunotherapy has successfully treated numerous cancers [19–21]. The FDA has approved Atezolizumab as the first immunotherapy for the treatment of TNBC. Atezolizumab is a humanized monoclonal antibody against the PD-L1 receptor that enhances T cell activity against cancer cells by blocking binding to the PD-L1 receptors PD-1 and B7.1. As a result, it is worthwhile to investigate immunotherapies and conduct clinical trials for treating TNBC patients with immunotherapy [22,23]. Nanoparticle-antibody conjugates have recently been used to develop diagnostic and imaging platforms for in vitro and in vivo applications. The combination of nanoparticles and antibodies improves in vitro diagnostics.

Here, we design an anti-PD-L1 antibody-carbon dots (CDs) conjugated system for immunotherapy and bioimaging TNBC cells. To achieve better CDs and coverage for theragnostic, we propose a covalent strategy - modifying CDs using ethylene glycol diamine (EGA) as a stabilizing agent and using an anti-PD-L1 antibody, as a target moiety. EGA-modified CDs (CDs-EGA) were covalently linked to antibodies. CDs bioconjugate. The surface characteristics of the modified CDs and CDs bioconjugate were evaluated. The effective internalization of anti-PD-L1

Antibody conjugated by CD was assessed using Fluorescence microscope in MDA-MB-231 cancer cells with high expression of PD-L1. Moreover, the toxic effect of anti-PD-L1-CD on proliferation and apoptosis was detected by colony assay and AO/EB assay, respectively [24]. The fluorescent results showed that anti-PD-L1-CD can effectively enter the cells. Furthermore, anti-PD-L1-CD showed greater cytotoxic effects on MDA-MB-231 cells compared to anti-PD-L1 antibodies.

Nitric acid (analytical grade 65 %), chitosan with medium molecular weight, glutaraldehyde (Glu) (grade 1, 50 % aqueous solution), polyethylene glycol diamine (PEG-diamine) (analytical grade, molecular weight: 148.20300), and sodium hydroxide were provided by Sigma-Aldrich Corporation. Zist Fanavaran Sina Corporation provided an Anti-PDL1 antibody. In addition, Iran's Genetic Bank provided MDA-MB-231 and HDF cells. Bio Idea Corporation provided Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) high glucose, l-glutamine, and trypsin 0.25 %. Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) was prepared from Cegrogen, and (4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole) (DAPI), (dimethylthizol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) powder, Ethidium Bromide and Acridine Orange were purchased from Denazist.

CDs were synthesized utilizing a process modified from a reported technique. Parameters, such as different acids, molar ratio of acid: chitosan, reaction temperature (T), reaction time (t), were concerned to optimize the experimental conditions by Taguchi design [25]. According to this 25 mL nitric acid (2.5 M) dissolved in ultrapure water in a 1:1 (v/v) ratio, and 0.05 g of chitosan was added to the solution. The resulting mixture then transferred to a 50 mL flask for reflux and heated for 5 h at 100 °C; and then slowly cooled to room temperature. The pH of the solution was adjusted to 7 using the NaOH solution. After centrifuging the solution for 10 min in 4000 rpm, the solution of synthesized CDs was stored at 4 °C [26].

For optical absorbance, UV–Vis spectrometer; [Beifen-Ruili Analytical Instrument] was used and [Twinfinite 200 microplate fluorimeter] were utilized for photoluminescence (PL). A transmission electron microscopy (TEM) system [JEM 2100 (HR)] was utilized to characterize the morphology of CDs at 800 kV and 250 kV accelerating voltages. A card Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA) was used for IR characterization of CDs. Size and dispersion coefficient of CDs using DLS spectrophotometer (Horiba, SZ-100S2, Japan) at 25 °C was determined.

MDA-MB-231 (breast cancer cells) and HDF (Dermal Fibroblasts) cell lines were purchased from the National Center of Genetic Iran. These cells were full-grown in DMEM (Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium) high glucose improved with 1 % penicillin-streptomycin and 10 % fetal bovine serum; they were incubated in moist condition with 5 % CO2 at 37 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioconjugation of anti-PDL1 antibody with CD by glutaraldehyde
ce:section.ce:para: Bioconjugation process of anti-PDL1 antibody with CD was performed according to the following protocol [27]. To obtain the stable CDs bioconjugation we performed two step experiments. In the first stage, Amide groups of CDs (1 mL) were activated by glutaraldehyde linker (200 μL, 5 % w/v) and reacted for 30 min under ambient condition and then centrifuged (5min, 10000 rpm). Polyethylene glycol diamine (100 μL, 0.003 mg/mL) was then added to the solution under continues stirring at room temperature for 20 min and centrifuged again (5 min, 10,000 rpm). To obtain a colloidal dispersion, glutaraldehyde (200 μl, 5 % w/v) was added, stirred and centrifuged for 30 min at room temperature. Pegilation allows conjugated system stay stable and NH2 groups of glutaraldehyde made antibody's conjugating easier [28]. In second step Anti-PDL1 antibody (50 mL, 0.0011 mg/mL) were added to obtained dispersion and incubated overnight. The anti-PDL1 antibody as CDs solution was kept in the dark condition at 4 °C until use.

The cell viability test was performed using, Colorimetric test evaluation, MTT (3-(4, 5 dimethylthiazol-2yl)-2, 5-diphenyl-tertazolium bromide) method to investigate the cytotoxic effect of CD, Anti-PDL-1 antibody and Anti-PDL-1-CD in vitro conditions. In this method, MDA-MB-231 and HDF cells (1 × 104 cells/well) were seeded in a 96-well plate. The culture medium was replaced after 24 h with CQD (50, 100, 200, 300, and 400 μg/mL), Anti-PDL-1 antibody and Anti-PDL-1-CD (50, 150, 250, and 500 μg/mL) various concentrations for 24 h. At first, the cells were washed with PBS and 20 μL of MTT (5 mg/mL in PBS) solution was added to them. The plate was incubated for 4 h at 37 °C. Finally, 100 μL of DMSO was added to the wells and after 15 min of continuous shaking, the absorbance was recorded at 570 nm. MTT assay was utilized to determine IC50 values.

For each group, the average variable level was calculated as Mean ± SD. All the results were analyzed using Graph Pad Prism (version 7.03) and ANOVA (Tukey's Post-hoc) analysis of variance was used to compare the means in the evaluation range of p ≤ 0.05.

To assess cellular uptake, MDA-MB-231 cells (5 × 103 cells/well) were seeded in a 6-well plate. Cells were treated after 24 h with Anti-PDL-1-CD and Anti-PDL-1 antibodies, and incubated for 24 h. To detect the position of the cells, the nuclei of the cells were stained utilizing 4,6-diamidine-2-phenylindole dihydrochloride (DAPI, 10 μg/mL), a fluorescence dye, and incubated for 10 min in the dark.

To perform colony assay, MDA-MB-231 cells (500 cells/well) were separately seeded in 6-well plates and exposed to Anti-PDL-1 antibody and Anti-PDL-1-CD for 24 h. The cells were washed after 10 days with cool PBS and treated with 4 % paraformaldehyde. Then, 0.1 % crystal violet solution was added to the plates for 10 min. The cells were washed and air-dried to the counting colonies.

In order to detect apoptotic cells, AO/EB staining was utilized. MDA-MB-231 cells were cultured in a 24-well plate (4 × 105 cells/well) and then incubated for 24 h. For 24 h, the cells were treated with Anti-PD-L1-CD and Anti-PDL-1 antibodies. The cells were then washed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), and 20 μL of the cell suspension was combined with 1 μL of AO-EB working solution (1 μL of AO and EB stocks plus 1 mL PBS); These cells were then evaluated by fluorescence microscopy. Green color represents living cells and orange color represents dead cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and component characterization of CD
ce:section.ce:para: The color of CD aqueous solution was golden yellow under visible light and blue under UV light (Fig. 1 b,c). The absorbance spectrum showed two evident bands at 210 nm and 307 nm. The absorption band between 200 nm and 220 nm is caused by the transition of π→π* of sp2 domains. An absorption peak between 250 and 350 nm was also observed, which is related to the n→π* transition of the carbonyl group [29]. The maximum fluorescence emission for CDs was observed at 462.5 nm (Fig. 1a).

According to (Fig. 2 a), the synthesized quantum dot carbon nanoparticles have a nature of small particles below 10 nm is evident in the figure and particles larger than 20 nm can also be identified, which shows that the synthesized quantum dot carbon particles are not separated from spherical shape and are homogeneously, these results conformed with DLS-Zeta sizer analyze, (Fig. 2b), It shows the particle size distribution in the range of 0.33–150 nm. As can be seen in the figure, the maximum peak in the area below 20 nm indicates the synthesis and distribution of fine particles (less than 10 nm) in the solution. From the appearance of only one peak in the graph, it can be concluded that it is a monodisperse sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characterization of the bioconjugate CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The anti-PD-L1 antibody was conjugated with CDs using Glutaraldehyde as a linker and Peg-diamine as a spacer. To find the optical amount, the fluorescence emission intensity was measured and compared. 200 μL of glutaraldehyde and 100 μL of PEG were chosen as the optimal amount (Fig. 3 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell compatibility of the CDs bioconjugate

ce:section.ce:para: (Fig. 4 A) shows the morphology of MDA-MB-231 and HDF cells cultured in a complete medium. The inhibitory impacts of CD, anti-PD-L1-CD, and anti-PD-L1 antibodies on the viability of HDF and MDA-MB-231 cells were investigated. According to obtained results, cell viability of HDF cells exposure to CD exceeded 95 % in 0–400 μg/mL concentrations of CD. As for MDA-MB-231 cells, cell growth rate was about to 100 % when added 0–400 μg/mL concentrations of CD (Fig. 4B). Therefore, free CD had no cytotoxic effect on the rate of growth and proliferation of MDA-MB-231 and HDF cells after 24 h. Furthermore, the different concentrations of anti-PD-L1 antibody and anti-PD-L1-CD revealed no toxic impact on the survival rate of HDF cells due to the lack of PDL-1 expression on the surface of HDF cells (Fig. 4C).

On the contrary, anti-PD-L1-CD and anti-PD-L1 antibody showed significant reduction on the survival rate of MDA-MB-231 cells (Fig. 4D). In addition, it was found that MDA-MB-231 cells treated with anti-PD-L1-CD has lower cell viability compared to MDA-MB-231 cells treated with PD-L1 antibody at similar concentrations (50, 150, 250, and 500 μg/mL). The dose of 250 μg/mL was employed to further compare the cytotoxic impacts of anti-PD-L1-CD and anti-PD-L1 antibodies.

Cellular uptake investigation is critical for cancer therapy due to whether drug can internalize into the treated cells. Fluorescence microscopy was used to investigate the uptake of anti-PD-L1-CD into MDA-MB-231 cells. Untreated and anti-PD-L1 antibody-treated MDA-MB-231 cells emitted no fluorescence light. In contrast, anti-PD-L1-CD treated MDA-MB-231 cells released green fluorescence owing to the presence of CDs.

In fact, assessment of cellular uptake of anti-PD-L1-CD into the cells, the first anti-PD-L1-CD treated cells checked for the presence of fluorescence before adding DAPI and green fluorescence observed owing to the presence of CDs. After that, the cells were stained by DAPI and the images were taken separately. Finally, the images were merged by Image J.

On the basis of merged images of MDA-MB-231 cells stained by DAPI, anti-PD-L1-CD could effectively be transferred into MDA-MB-231 cells after 24-h incubation. Since CDs can easily transit the cell membrane and accumulate in cell cytosol or nucleus owing to their size and properties, therefore green fluorescence is emitted around cell membrane [30]. In accordance with previous studies [31,32], our findings demonstrated green fluorescence nearby DAPI-stained nuclei of MDA-MB-231 cells. It suggests that CDs are able to help to effective internalization of anti-PD-L1 antibody into cancer cells and recognize cancer cells. Kong et al. indicated that CD can help the accumulation of doxorubicin in MCF‐7 cell nucleus and improve the therapeutic and cytotoxic effects (Fig. 5 ).

Under normal conditions, each cancer cell can form a colony with at least 50 cells due to its high proliferation rate. Therefore, the colony test was used to evaluate the effects of anti-PD-L1 antibody and anti-PD-L1-CD in inhibiting the colony formation by MDA-MB-231 cells within 10 days. As illustrated in (Fig. 6 ), anti-PD-L1 antibody exposed MDA-MB-231 cells demonstrated effective reduction in colony forming compared to untreated cells. Furthermore, anti-PD-L1-CD could show further inhibitory effects on colony forming of MDA-MB-231 cells compared to anti-PD-L1 antibody exposed cells.

Apoptosis is a type of programmed cell death that occurs in multicellular organisms and it can occur in cells where anticancer agents have been used. In this study, the AO/EB method was used to investigate apoptosis as a possible mechanism to inhibit growth and induce death in anti-PD-L1 antibody and anti-PD-L1-CD exposed to MDA-MB-231 cells. Living cells showed a green fluorescent color due to the entry of AO, and dead or damaged cells showed a red-orange fluorescent color due to the entry of EB. In the case of AO/EB assay, although released green fluorescent emitted by CD can lead to an interference with emitted green fluorescent color due to the entry of AO, emitted red-orange fluorescent color owing to the entry of EB is considered as an important factor for this assay because EB can stain only cells that have lost membrane integrity. Therefore, it will stain all dead cells to generate red fluorescence. Indeed, a red-orange fluorescence caused by the entry of anti-PD-L1-CD shows cell death.

According to the data, in the control group apoptotic cell percentage was insignificant, and the MDA-MB-231 cells were standard. As illustrated in merged images, green and orange-red fluorescence were detected in anti-PD-L1 antibody-subjected MDA-MB-231 cells and percentage of apoptotic death indicated by orange fluorescence was about 13 %. MDA-MB-231 cells subjected to anti-PD-L1-CD exhibited a higher percentage of apoptotic death compared to anti-PD-L1 antibody-subjected MDA-MB-231 cells, which indicates a significant reduction in cell viability and more cell death in this group. Further apoptosis induced by anti-PD-L1-CD in MDA-MB-231 cells can be due to improving of internalization of anti-PD-L1-CD. Obtained data were in line with those of Yang et al. which expressed CD can effectively deliver drug to cancer cells (Fig. 7 ).

Despite the existing treatment methods for (TNBC) and the weaknesses of these methods, such limitations require the strengthening of new diagnostic and treatment methods. With the progress of nanotechnology in biomedical sciences in recent decades in the field of contrast agents and drug delivery carriers, scientists have been drawn to the use of new approaches for targeted and simultaneous delivery of diagnostic and therapeutic agents. To summarize, in this research, the novel conjugated system with theragnostic nanoparticles (NPs) that improve cancer diagnosis and therapeutic effects were investigated. The bioconjugate of CDs forming carbon dots was prepared as an imaging agent and anti-PDL1 antibody as a targeting and immunotherapy agent. We investigated whether CD conjugation with anti-PD-L1 antibody can use for bioimaging and improve cytotoxic effects of anti-PD-L1 antibodies on MDA-MB-231 cells. Based on our results, the presence of green fluorescent around nuclei stained by DAPI demonstrates that anti-PD-L1-CD could effectively enter cells. Furthermore, anti-PD-L1-CD revealed more cytotoxic effects compared to anti-PD-L1 antibodies on MDA-MB-231 cells.
The authors declare no conﬂict of interest.
Not Applicable.
Not Applicable.

M. Azizi: Investigation, Writing – original draft. A. Sheini: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Mir Saeed Seyed Dorraji: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. H. Alidadi: Methodology, Writing – original draft. E. Fekri: Investigation, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the University of Zanjan and Shahid Chamran University of Ahvaz for their financial and other supports.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes in breast cancer and implications for clinical practice
